courseMetadata.title: Front-End System Design: Architecting for Scale
courseMetadata.description: A staff-level course for senior frontend and fullstack engineers who need to move from building features to architecting whole front-end systems. Covers requirements elicitation, structural styles (monoliths, modular monoliths, micro-frontends), rendering and data-fetching strategy, state architecture, design systems and shared platform concerns, performance and scalability engineering, and how to document and defend architectural decisions.

prerequisites.description: Several years of professional experience building production front-end applications with a modern framework (React, Vue, Angular, or similar).
Working familiarity with REST and/or GraphQL APIs and general client-server web architecture.
Basic exposure to how build tooling (bundlers, code-splitting) affects a shipped application, even without deep architecture experience.

interleavingGroups.rationaleNote: Forces learners to re-evaluate the same rendering-strategy decision twice: first purely on SEO/freshness/personalization grounds (S3), then under a cost/scale lens where the same route can look different once a performance budget is attached (S6). Contrast near-transfer of one decision viewed through two lenses.

Interleaves structural-style selection with governance-model design so learners repeatedly apply Conway's Law and coordination-cost reasoning in two different artifacts (a structural decision vs. a governance proposal), preventing the pattern from being memorized as a single-use checklist.
Directly chains a data-fetching pattern decision into its downstream state-architecture consequences, reinforcing that the two competencies are not independently decidable.

Applies the same centralization test (introduced with design systems in S5) to a different set of concerns (observability, error boundaries, feature flags) in S6, testing whether the underlying heuristic transferred rather than a specific example being memorized.

Deliberately co-located in the capstone: documentation quality and trade-off reasoning quality are graded together because a well-reasoned decision that isn't documented with context-specific rejection reasoning fails the same bar as a poorly-reasoned one.

probeQuestions.prompt: A stakeholder tells you 'we need the app to support offline mode.' Which best describes this requirement, and where does it primarily belong in your planning?
options: It's a functional requirement, and it's purely a backend/infrastructure concern
It's a non-functional requirement, and it directly constrains front-end decisions like data-caching architecture
It's a functional requirement, and it can be deferred until after the UI is built
It's not a real requirement until it's written in a formal spec document

optionFeedback.feedback: Offline support is a quality attribute (how well the system behaves under a condition), not a capability itself, and it has direct front-end implications for caching and state architecture.
Correct. Offline support is a non-functional requirement, and it must be handled by the front-end team directly since it shapes caching and state decisions from the start.
NFRs like offline support are expensive to retrofit; deferring them until after the UI is built typically means re-architecting rather than adding a feature.
A requirement is real once it constrains a decision, regardless of documentation format; waiting for formal paperwork risks losing decision-critical context.

scoringCriteria: Correctly classifies the requirement as non-functional

routingRules.action: flag-for-review: consider lighter pacing through s1-foundations-requirements
flag-for-review: ensure s1-foundations-requirements is not skipped or accelerated

probeQuestions.prompt: A 40-person company has one product and four feature teams that frequently need to change shared UI simultaneously, but has no separate release cadences or compliance boundaries between teams. What's the best-justified architecture style?
options: Micro-frontends, because they are more scalable than a monolith by default
A modular monolith, because teams need real ownership boundaries but haven't yet earned the coordination cost of independent deployability
A pure monolith with no internal module boundaries, since it's simplest
Micro-frontends, using Module Federation, since it's the modern standard

optionFeedback.feedback: This treats micro-frontends as inherently more scalable regardless of context, which ignores the coordination cost they introduce when it isn't offset by a real need for independent deployability.
Correct. This scenario needs enforced ownership boundaries but doesn't show evidence of the deploy-cadence or compliance separation that would justify paying for micro-frontend composition costs.
A pure monolith without module boundaries doesn't give teams the ownership clarity the scenario says they need, risking collisions between four teams changing shared UI.
This treats Module Federation as synonymous with 'the micro-frontend decision' and skips evaluating whether decomposition is justified at all, or which composition mechanism would fit best.

scoringCriteria: Justifies via coordination-cost-vs-composition-cost reasoning rather than trend

routingRules.action: unlock-advanced: offer extended case studies in s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs
flag-for-review: reinforce coordination-cost framework before s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs

probeQuestions.prompt: True or false, as a general architectural principle: server-side rendering (SSR) is strictly better for performance than client-side rendering (CSR).
options: True — SSR should be the default choice whenever performance matters
False — each rendering strategy shifts when and where rendering cost is paid; the right choice depends on the route's SEO, freshness, and personalization needs
True — but only for e-commerce sites specifically
False — CSR is always faster than SSR regardless of context

optionFeedback.feedback: This is the named misconception that SSR is strictly better; it ignores that SSR pays a recurring per-request server cost, which is wasteful for infrequently-changing content.
Correct. Rendering strategy is workload-dependent, and mature architectures typically mix strategies per route rather than picking one globally.
Restricting the claim to e-commerce doesn't fix the underlying error — the workload-dependence applies across domains, not just one industry.
This overcorrects into an equally absolute claim in the other direction; CSR has its own costs (slower first paint, weaker SEO) that make it wrong for some routes.

scoringCriteria: Articulates workload-dependence as the deciding factor

routingRules.action: skip-to-section: allow accelerated pace through s3-rendering-data-strategies rendering-strategy portion

probeQuestions.prompt: Your app fetches a user's order history from an API and stores it in the same global store as a boolean tracking whether a dropdown menu is open, updated using the same pattern. What's the most likely consequence?
options: None — a global store is designed to hold any kind of state equally well
The order history can go stale, since it has no distinct invalidation/refetch strategy the way genuinely owned UI state does
The dropdown state will leak between different users' browser sessions
This is technically impossible; global stores cannot hold API response data

optionFeedback.feedback: This ignores the fundamental lifecycle difference between client/UI state (which the app owns outright) and server-cache state (borrowed from a server source of truth that can change independently).
Correct. Server-cache state needs an explicit staleness/invalidation strategy; treating it like ordinary state variables is the most common cause of stale-data bugs.
Session leakage is a different, unrelated concern from the state-type conflation problem being tested here.
Technically a store can hold anything; the problem is conceptual and architectural, not a technical limitation.

scoringCriteria: Distinguishes client/UI state from server-cache state

routingRules.action: unlock-advanced: offer extended cross-module state-sharing case in s4-state-management-architecture
flag-for-review: reinforce state-type distinction before s4-state-management-architecture

probeQuestions.prompt: A team has published a polished shared component library. Is that sufficient for a functioning design system?
options: Yes — a component library is what a design system fundamentally is
No — a full design system also needs design tokens, a governance process, and a distribution/versioning strategy, or consuming teams will drift out of sync
Yes, as long as the components are visually polished
No — a design system additionally requires every product-specific component to live in the shared library

optionFeedback.feedback: This is the named misconception that a design system is 'just' a component library; it omits the governance and distribution pieces that prevent drift.
Correct. Without tokens, governance, and a distribution strategy, a component library drifts out of sync with real usage within months.
Visual polish doesn't address the sync/governance problem; a beautiful but ungoverned library still drifts.
Forcing all product-specific components into the shared system is actually a common overreach that bloats the system, not a requirement for it to function.

scoringCriteria: Names at least two of: tokens, governance, distribution

routingRules.action: unlock-advanced: offer extended governance case study in s5-design-systems-shared-platform

probeQuestions.prompt: When should performance budgets (e.g., max JS bundle size, target load time) be established relative to the architecture decisions that affect them?
options: After launch, as part of a dedicated optimization sprint
Before or during structural and rendering decisions, since budgets constrain those decisions the same way other non-functional requirements do
Only when a Lighthouse score drops below a threshold in CI
Performance budgets are not useful for front-end architecture decisions

optionFeedback.feedback: By the time you're 'optimizing' post-launch, the expensive structural decisions (bundling, composition, rendering) are already locked in, and real gains usually require revisiting architecture, not just code.
Correct. Treating performance budgets as inputs to structural and rendering decisions — not a downstream check — is what prevents expensive rework.
Reacting only to CI score drops treats performance as a lagging indicator instead of an upfront constraint, and misses that a single lab score isn't a complete signal anyway.
Performance budgets are directly useful as architectural constraints — they inform composition and rendering strategy the same way SEO or compliance requirements do.

scoringCriteria: Places budgets before/during structural decisions, not after launch

routingRules.action: unlock-advanced: offer extended budget-traceback case in s6-performance-scalability-resilience
flag-for-review: reinforce 'performance is architectural' framing before s6-performance-scalability-resilience

probeQuestions.prompt: In two to three sentences: what makes an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) more valuable six months later than a simple summary of what was chosen?
scoringCriteria: Mentions that alternatives considered and rejected are documented, not just the final choice
Mentions that rejection reasoning is specific to context, enabling the decision to be re-evaluated later if that context changes

routingRules.action: unlock-advanced: offer extended ADR case study in s7-documenting-deciding-capstone
flag-for-review: ensure s7-documenting-deciding-capstone ADR anatomy section is not skipped

competencies.title: Requirements Elicitation for Front-End Systems
competencies.description: Translating business, product, and non-functional constraints (scale, team size, SEO, compliance, device targets) into architecture-relevant requirements, and distinguishing decision-critical unknowns from merely interesting ones.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a business scenario, the learner produces a categorized list of functional and non-functional requirements and correctly identifies which are decision-critical.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Non-functional requirements (scale, SEO, offline support) are a backend concern.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: NFRs directly determine front-end decisions such as rendering strategy and caching architecture; if the front-end team doesn't gather them directly, the system is designed against stale or missing constraints.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Re-present a scenario where an NFR was gathered only by the backend team and show the specific front-end architecture failure that resulted; have the learner re-derive the correct elicitation step.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: More requirements-gathering always produces a better architecture.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Elicitation has a real time cost; continuing past the point where an answer would change the architecture is avoidance of deciding under uncertainty, not rigor.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Give the learner a requirements list with both decision-critical and non-decision-critical items mixed together and have them justify a cutoff, with feedback on any items they over-included.

competencies.title: Front-End Architecture Styles
competencies.description: Comparing monoliths, modular monoliths, and micro-frontends as structural options with distinct trade-offs, chosen by coordination cost vs. composition cost rather than trend.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a scenario describing team size, org shape, and compliance/release-cadence facts, the learner selects and defends a specific architecture style using the coordination-cost-vs-composition-cost framework.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Micro-frontends are simply 'microservices for the front end' and are always more scalable.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: The analogy breaks down because UI must be composed into a single coherent experience the user perceives as one product, introducing shared-UX and shared-runtime costs backend microservices don't have.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner list the composition costs (shared design language, duplicate framework risk, consistent routing) that have no backend-microservices analog, then re-evaluate a scenario they previously mis-scored.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A monolith is always the 'legacy' or inferior choice.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: For small-to-mid teams a modular monolith is frequently the higher-leverage, lower-risk option because it avoids paying composition costs with no offsetting coordination benefit.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a small-team scenario and ask the learner to compute what coordination pain a micro-frontend split would actually avoid; when the answer is 'none,' reinforce that decomposition cost was paid for nothing.

competencies.title: Micro-Frontend Composition Strategies
competencies.description: Selecting and justifying composition mechanisms (build-time, runtime/client-side, edge-side/server-side, Module Federation) once decomposition itself is justified.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a scenario requiring decomposition, the learner selects a specific composition mechanism (build-time, client-side runtime, or edge/server-side) and justifies it against the scenario's SEO, deployability, and infrastructure constraints.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Module Federation is the only real way to do micro-frontends.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: It is one runtime-composition technique among several (build-time composition, iframe/web-component runtime composition, edge-side includes), each with different trade-offs in operational cost and independence.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present three scenarios each best served by a different composition category, and require the learner to name the mechanism and reject Module Federation explicitly where it isn't the best fit.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Composition strategy is a purely technical choice.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: It is inseparable from team ownership boundaries and deployment cadence; a technically elegant composition mechanism that ignores who owns what will still create coordination friction.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner re-derive a composition choice after being given org/ownership facts they were previously missing, showing how the 'purely technical' answer changes.

competencies.title: Rendering & Delivery Strategies
competencies.description: Choosing among CSR, SSR, SSG, ISR, and streaming/partial hydration on a per-route basis based on SEO, freshness, and personalization constraints.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a multi-route application scenario, the learner assigns a justified rendering strategy per route and explains the SEO/freshness/personalization reasoning for each.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: SSR is strictly 'better' than CSR for performance.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: SSR shifts cost to a recurring per-request server compute cost and can hurt TTFB/server load at scale; the right answer is workload-dependent, not universally in SSR's favor.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a low-change-frequency, high-traffic route and have the learner calculate why paying per-request SSR cost is wasteful compared to SSG, correcting the 'SSR is always better' instinct with a concrete cost comparison.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: You pick one rendering strategy for the whole app.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Modern architectures mix strategies per route or per component (islands, partial hydration, streaming); a single global choice systematically under-serves some routes and over-spends on others.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Give the learner a mixed-requirement application and require a per-route table rather than a single answer, with corrective feedback on any route forced into an ill-fitting global choice.

competencies.title: API & Backend Integration Patterns
competencies.description: Designing the data-access layer: BFF, GraphQL vs. REST, aggregation, and contract ownership across teams.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a multi-team, multi-client scenario, the learner proposes a data-access architecture (BFF, GraphQL, or both) and specifies contract ownership.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: GraphQL always reduces over-fetching problems that REST can't solve.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: REST with well-designed aggregation/BFF layers can solve the same problems, and GraphQL introduces its own caching and schema-complexity costs.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a REST+BFF solution that already solves an over-fetching problem and ask the learner to identify what, if anything, GraphQL would add in that specific case.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The BFF pattern is redundant if you already have GraphQL.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A BFF and a GraphQL gateway solve overlapping but distinct problems: client-specific shaping/aggregation versus letting a client express exactly the fields it needs from a federated schema.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner design a scenario with two very different client types (mobile, desktop dashboard) sharing one GraphQL schema and identify where a thin BFF layer per client is still needed.

competencies.title: State & Data Management Architecture
competencies.description: Architecting client state, server-cache state, and cross-app state sharing (or deliberately not sharing it) with lifecycle-appropriate tooling.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a list of on-screen state items, the learner classifies each as client/UI or server-cache state and proposes lifecycle-appropriate handling, including cross-module sharing where applicable.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Server-cache data and client/UI state should be managed with the same tool/pattern.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Treating remote data as ordinary state causes staleness, duplication, and sync bugs; server-cache state needs explicit invalidation/refetch handling that plain state variables don't provide.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk the learner through a state audit where a piece of server-cache data was mismanaged as UI state, and have them re-classify it with the correct handling strategy.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Global state should be minimized to almost nothing, as a universal rule.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: This is context-dependent: some state (e.g., authenticated user identity) legitimately belongs at the top of the tree; the real rule is scoping state to the smallest region that needs it, which is sometimes the whole app.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a case where over-applying the 'minimize global state' rule caused painful prop-drilling, and have the learner identify the state that should have been global from the start.

competencies.title: Design Systems & Component Architecture
competencies.description: Structuring shared component libraries, design tokens, and versioning/distribution strategy across teams, including governance models that scale with team count.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a multi-team scenario, the learner structures a component-library layering (tokens/primitives/composed/product-specific) and proposes a governance and distribution model appropriate to the team count and structure.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A design system is just a shared component library.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: It also encodes design tokens, governance, versioning, and cross-team contribution process, which are architectural concerns; skipping these means the component library alone can't prevent drift.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a component library that drifted out of sync within months due to missing governance, and have the learner identify which of the three missing pieces would have prevented it.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Once built, a design system stays in sync automatically.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Without a distribution and versioning strategy, consuming apps drift immediately since nothing forces or nudges them to adopt updates.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner choose between versioned-package and live/shared-runtime distribution for a specific scenario and justify the trade-off in terms of consumer control vs. automatic consistency.

competencies.title: Performance & Scalability Engineering
competencies.description: Applying bundling, code-splitting, caching, and Core Web Vitals budgets as architectural constraints traced back to composition and rendering decisions, not treated as a post-launch pass.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a performance budget and an existing composition/rendering architecture, the learner traces the budget back to the specific structural decisions that would need to change to meet it.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Performance is a QA/optimization pass done at the end.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Bundling, splitting, and caching decisions are architectural and become expensive to retrofit; by launch, the structural decisions that determine performance are typically already locked in.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a post-launch performance problem caused by an early composition decision and have the learner identify that no amount of late-stage optimization could fully fix it without revisiting that decision.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A single Lighthouse score is a sufficient performance signal.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Core Web Vitals and field data (RUM) reflect real-device and real-network diversity that lab scores can hide, so a strong lab score can coexist with a poor real-user experience.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a scenario with a high lab score but poor RUM data and have the learner explain the gap and propose using RUM as the ongoing source of truth alongside lab scores as a CI gate.

competencies.title: Cross-Cutting Platform Concerns
competencies.description: Planning for auth, observability, error boundaries, feature flags, and i18n as shared architectural responsibilities that need platform-level coordination in multi-module systems.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a multi-module architecture, the learner identifies which cross-cutting concerns require centralized platform-level coordination versus which can be safely left to individual teams, using an explicit centralization test.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Auth, observability, and feature flags are 'plumbing' that can be bolted onto any architecture later.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: They need to be planned alongside composition/rendering choices, or they force painful rework once modules and teams are already independently operating.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a scenario where a cross-cutting concern was deferred and had to be retrofitted across five independently deployed modules, and quantify the rework compared to planning it upfront.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Each micro-frontend can implement these concerns independently without coordination.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Inconsistent implementations fragment UX and create security/observability blind spots, and prevent tracing a single incident across module boundaries.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present an incident scenario where independent per-module logging prevented diagnosing a cross-module failure, and have the learner propose the shared convention that would have prevented it.

competencies.title: Team Topology & Organizational Alignment
competencies.description: Reasoning about Conway's Law and team boundaries as inputs to, and outputs of, architecture decisions, including anticipating team shape 12-18 months out.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given an org structure (current and planned), the learner explains, in both directions of Conway's Law, how it constrains or is constrained by a candidate architecture.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Architecture should be chosen purely on technical merit, then teams reorganize to fit it.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Existing team structure constrains what architectures are realistically adoptable right now; Conway's Law runs in both directions, not just org-shapes-architecture.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a technically 'ideal' architecture proposed for a team with no plans to split, and have the learner identify the adoption failure that would result and propose a better-fitted alternative.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Micro-frontends automatically enable team autonomy.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Without clear contracts and shared platform investment, they can create more cross-team coupling, not less, because teams still depend on each other's runtime behavior and shared conventions.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a case where a micro-frontend split increased coupling due to missing contracts, and have the learner identify what platform investment (contracts, shared conventions) was missing.

competencies.title: Architecture Documentation & Communication
competencies.description: Producing ADRs, diagrams, and RFC-style proposals that communicate and defend architectural decisions, with explicit rejection reasoning and labeled system boundaries.

desiredOutcomes.description: The learner authors an ADR (context, decision, rejected alternatives with context-specific reasoning, consequences) and a diagram with explicit ownership and data-flow-direction labels for a given scenario.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Documentation is a summary written after the decision is made.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Effective ADRs capture the alternatives considered and rejected, which is where most of the future value lives; a summary of only the chosen option can't be re-evaluated later.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Give the learner a summary-only 'ADR' and have them rewrite it by adding a rejected-alternatives section with context-specific (not generic) reasoning.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A diagram is self-explanatory.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Diagrams without stated boundaries, ownership, and data-flow direction create ambiguity and false alignment between readers who each interpret it differently.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show two different readers' interpretations of the same unlabeled diagram, then have the learner add ownership and data-flow-direction labels that resolve the ambiguity.

competencies.title: Trade-off Synthesis & Decision-Making
competencies.description: Weighing competing architectural forces against a specific context to reach and justify a defensible decision, integrating nearly every other competency in the course.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a full, novel scenario, the learner synthesizes decisions across structure, rendering, data, state, and shared platform into one coherent, context-justified proposal, explicitly rejecting at least one plausible alternative per major decision.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: There is a single 'best practice' architecture that applies regardless of context.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Nearly every front-end architecture decision is a trade-off that shifts with team size, timeline, and product constraints; a 'best practice' framing hides the context-dependence that actually determines the right answer.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present the same surface-level scenario twice with different team/traffic/compliance facts and have the learner show that the correct architecture differs, correcting any 'one right answer' response.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: More technology/flexibility is always safer.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Optionality has a real cost in complexity and cognitive load that must be weighed against its benefit; defaulting to maximum flexibility is not a substitute for evidence-based reasoning about actual constraints.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a small, well-understood project where a learner chose a maximally flexible architecture 'to be safe,' and have them quantify the ongoing complexity cost against the actual (low) need for that flexibility.

questions.prompt: A team building a patient-portal app is told the app must support screen readers due to a legal requirement in their market. Which statement best reflects how this should be handled?
options: It's a backend compliance issue and doesn't need to shape front-end component architecture
It should be planned into the component architecture from the start, since it constrains how components are built, not just polished at the end
It only matters once the legal team formally signs off on launch
It's a nice-to-have that can be addressed if time allows after core features ship

optionFeedback.feedback: Accessibility is a front-end-relevant non-functional requirement; treating it as backend-only means the front-end team designs against a stale picture of constraints.
Correct. Accessibility requirements constrain component architecture directly and are expensive to retrofit as a late 'polish pass.'
Waiting for formal sign-off risks discovering the constraint too late to design for it cheaply; it should shape architecture from elicitation, not from a launch gate.
Legally mandated requirements aren't optional 'if time allows' items; deferring them is exactly how they end up forcing rework.

successCriteria: Identifies accessibility as a front-end-architecture-shaping NFR
Rejects deferring it to a later phase

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats a stated NFR as belonging exclusively to backend/compliance teams and outside front-end scope.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s1-foundations-requirements worked example (furniture retailer) and require the learner to re-map the NFR to its constrained architectural decision category.

questions.prompt: You're eliciting requirements for a new internal analytics dashboard for a 6-person team. In 3-4 sentences, explain which two or three questions you'd prioritize as decision-critical, and one question you would deliberately NOT chase down at this stage, with your reasoning for the cutoff.
successCriteria: Identifies questions that would actually change the architecture if answered differently (e.g., expected team growth, external vs. internal-only access)
Names a non-decision-critical question and justifies excluding it based on time cost vs. decision impact
Does not simply list requirements without distinguishing decision-critical from merely interesting

rubric.excellent: Clearly distinguishes decision-critical from non-decision-critical questions with specific reasoning tied to how each would change the architecture; explicitly names a cutoff and justifies it.
rubric.adequate: Identifies reasonable decision-critical questions but reasoning for the cutoff is vague or generic rather than tied to a specific architectural consequence.
rubric.weak: Lists requirements or questions without distinguishing decision-critical from merely interesting, or provides no reasoning for excluding anything.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats all gathered requirements as equally important and does not propose a stopping point.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s1-foundations-requirements activity distinguishing decision-critical vs. interesting unknowns; require a forced ranking exercise.

questions.prompt: A 300-person marketplace has three functionally and organizationally separate teams with different compliance regimes and release cadences. Which architecture style is best justified?
options: A monolith, because it's simpler to reason about for everyone
Micro-frontends, because the coordination cost of a shared pipeline is real and measured, and the teams are already organizationally separate
Micro-frontends, because they are always more scalable than a monolith
A modular monolith, because it's a safer middle ground regardless of context

optionFeedback.feedback: This ignores that a monolith would force genuinely separate teams with different compliance regimes into a shared deploy pipeline, creating real, avoidable coordination pain.
Correct. Existing organizational separation plus measurable coordination cost justifies paying the composition cost of micro-frontends.
This treats micro-frontends as inherently more scalable rather than justified by this scenario's specific facts.
'Safer middle ground regardless of context' isn't a real justification; the scenario's separate compliance regimes and cadences point past modular monolith toward genuine decomposition.

successCriteria: Selects micro-frontends
Justifies via existing organizational separation and measured coordination cost

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects or rejects micro-frontends based on general scalability reputation rather than scenario-specific coordination-cost evidence.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs three-company worked example and require the learner to compute the coordination-cost-vs-composition-cost ratio explicitly.

errorPatterns.description: Learner defaults to treating a monolith as inferior even in low-coordination-need scenarios.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the small-startup case from s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs and require the learner to identify the absent coordination pain.

questions.prompt: A 40-person company with one product and four collaborating feature teams is proposing a full micro-frontend split, citing 'team autonomy' as the goal. In 3-5 sentences, evaluate this proposal using the coordination-cost-vs-composition-cost framework and recommend an alternative if appropriate.
successCriteria: Questions whether real coordination pain (deploy conflicts, compliance separation) currently exists to justify the composition cost
Recommends a modular monolith or similar lower-cost alternative if the scenario doesn't show sufficient justification
Explicitly ties reasoning to this scenario's facts rather than a general rule

rubric.excellent: Explicitly applies the coordination-cost-vs-composition-cost framework to this scenario's specific facts, identifies that the described coordination pain doesn't clearly justify full decomposition, and proposes a specific lower-cost alternative with reasoning.
rubric.adequate: Recommends a reasonable alternative but reasoning is more generic ('monoliths are simpler') than tied to this scenario's specific coordination evidence.
rubric.weak: Accepts the micro-frontend proposal at face value because it's framed as promoting 'autonomy,' without evaluating whether coordination cost actually justifies it.

errorPatterns.description: Learner accepts a micro-frontend proposal on rhetorical grounds ('autonomy,' 'modern') without evaluating actual coordination-cost evidence in the scenario.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs Company B worked example (modular monolith case) as a direct parallel.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes Module Federation's relationship to 'micro-frontends' as a concept?
options: They are synonyms - Module Federation is the only real way to build micro-frontends
Module Federation is one client-side runtime composition technique among several broader composition categories
Module Federation is a backend microservices tool unrelated to front-end composition
Module Federation replaces the need to decide on a composition strategy at all

optionFeedback.feedback: This ignores build-time and edge/server-side composition as valid alternatives with their own trade-offs.
Correct. It's one client-side runtime technique among build-time, client-side runtime, and edge/server-side composition.
Module Federation is specifically a front-end, Webpack/Rspack-based runtime composition tool, not a backend concept.
Choosing Module Federation IS choosing a composition strategy; it doesn't remove that decision, it makes it.

successCriteria: Correctly classifies Module Federation as one of several composition categories

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats Module Federation as synonymous with 'doing micro-frontends' rather than one composition option.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs composition-strategies section listing all three categories with trade-offs.

questions.prompt: Two engineers are choosing a composition mechanism for a new micro-frontend split. One says 'let's just pick whichever is technically cleanest.' In 3-4 sentences, explain what's missing from that framing and what additional information should shape the decision.
successCriteria: States that composition strategy is inseparable from team ownership boundaries and deployment cadence
Identifies at least one concrete organizational fact (ownership, release cadence) that should inform the choice

rubric.excellent: Clearly explains that composition strategy is not purely technical, names deployment cadence and/or ownership boundaries as necessary inputs, and gives a concrete example of how a different org fact would change the recommended mechanism.
rubric.adequate: Identifies that organizational factors matter but doesn't tie them concretely to how the choice of mechanism would change.
rubric.weak: Treats the choice as purely a matter of technical cleanliness or performance, missing the organizational dimension entirely.

errorPatterns.description: Learner evaluates composition mechanisms purely on technical merit without considering team ownership/deployment cadence.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs Conway's Law discussion and require the learner to re-answer given explicit ownership facts.

questions.prompt: A marketing homepage changes content roughly once a month and must be indexable and load instantly for high-volume ad traffic. Which rendering strategy best fits?
options: SSR, because server-rendering is always the safest, most reliable option
CSR, because it's the cheapest to host
SSG, because the content changes infrequently, so pre-rendering once at build time serves it fastest with no per-request cost
You should pick one strategy for the whole site and apply it everywhere, including this page

optionFeedback.feedback: This ignores the recurring per-request server cost SSR would incur for content this static, where SSG would avoid that cost entirely.
CSR risks poor first-paint speed and SEO reliability, conflicting with the stated 'indexable and load instantly' requirement.
Correct. Low-change-frequency, SEO-critical content is best served by pre-rendering once at build time.
This is the misconception that a single global rendering strategy should apply to an entire application, ignoring per-route requirements.

successCriteria: Selects SSG
Justifies via low change frequency and SEO/speed needs

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner defaults to SSR as the 'safe' choice regardless of content change frequency.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s3-rendering-data-strategies rendering-spectrum lecture section and require a cost comparison exercise.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes a single rendering strategy for an entire multi-route application.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s3-rendering-data-strategies three-route worked example and require a per-route table.

questions.prompt: An authenticated user dashboard shows personalized, frequently-changing data and is not indexed by search engines. In 2-3 sentences, justify a rendering strategy for this route and explain why the reasoning differs from a public marketing page.
successCriteria: Recommends CSR (or SSR only if justified by measured first-paint priority)
Explains that lack of SEO need and personalization removes the case for pre-rendering

rubric.excellent: Correctly recommends CSR (or conditionally SSR with clear justification) and explicitly explains that the absence of SEO need and presence of personalization changes the calculus compared to a public page.
rubric.adequate: Recommends a reasonable strategy but the contrast with the public-page reasoning is underdeveloped.
rubric.weak: Applies the same reasoning used for the public marketing page without adjusting for personalization/no-SEO-need.

errorPatterns.description: Learner applies SSG/SSR reasoning meant for public, indexable content to a personalized, authenticated route.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s3-rendering-data-strategies dashboard portion of the three-route worked example.

questions.prompt: A team has adopted GraphQL and concludes they no longer need a BFF layer. What is the flaw in this reasoning?
options: GraphQL and BFFs cannot be used in the same architecture at all
GraphQL solves how a client expresses exactly what data it needs, while a BFF solves client-specific shaping and aggregation - removing one doesn't remove the need for the other
BFFs are obsolete technology that GraphQL was specifically designed to replace
GraphQL cannot reduce over-fetching, so this concern is irrelevant either way

optionFeedback.feedback: They are frequently combined - e.g., a GraphQL gateway acting as the BFF.
Correct. These are related but distinct problems; adopting one doesn't remove the need for the other.
There's no such intended replacement relationship between the two patterns historically or architecturally.
GraphQL does address over/under-fetching; the flaw in the team's reasoning is elsewhere, in conflating that with client-specific shaping.

successCriteria: Identifies the distinct problems each pattern solves

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner assumes GraphQL alone eliminates any need for client-specific data shaping.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s3-rendering-data-strategies BFF-vs-GraphQL section and require identifying a scenario where both are still needed together.

errorPatterns.description: Learner assumes REST cannot solve over-fetching without GraphQL.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface REST+BFF aggregation example from s3-rendering-data-strategies.

questions.prompt: A company has a mobile app and a desktop dashboard sharing one underlying GraphQL schema, but the mobile team complains payloads are still too large for their needs. In 3-4 sentences, propose a solution and explain why it doesn't contradict having adopted GraphQL.
successCriteria: Proposes a thin BFF or client-specific query-shaping layer for mobile
Explains this complements rather than contradicts the GraphQL schema

rubric.excellent: Proposes a specific client-shaping solution (e.g., a mobile-specific BFF or persisted/scoped queries) and clearly explains why this complements rather than replaces the GraphQL layer.
rubric.adequate: Proposes a reasonable solution but the explanation of why it doesn't contradict GraphQL adoption is thin.
rubric.weak: Concludes GraphQL adoption alone should have solved this and doesn't propose an additional client-shaping mechanism.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats persistent payload-size complaints as evidence GraphQL 'isn't working' rather than recognizing a missing client-shaping layer.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s3-rendering-data-strategies contract-ownership discussion.

questions.prompt: Which statement best reflects the correct position on global state?
options: Global state should always be minimized to nearly zero, in every application
Global state should be scoped to the smallest region that legitimately needs it - which is sometimes the whole app
Global state is always preferable to local component state for maintainability
Global state and server-cache state should always be stored in the exact same store

optionFeedback.feedback: This treats minimization as a universal rule rather than a context-dependent heuristic.
Correct. This matches the actual scoping rule: smallest region that legitimately needs it, which can be the whole app for things like auth identity.
This overcorrects in the opposite direction and isn't supported by the lecture's scoping rule.
This directly contradicts the core distinction between state types and their different lifecycle needs.

successCriteria: Selects the scoping-based rule over the universal-minimization rule

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner conflates client/UI state and server-cache state as requiring identical handling.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s4-state-management-architecture ride-sharing state-audit worked example.

errorPatterns.description: Learner applies 'minimize global state' as an absolute rule rather than a scoping heuristic.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s4-state-management-architecture global-state scoping discussion.

questions.prompt: A company splits into micro-frontends and a developer proposes sharing one global JavaScript store across all modules 'for convenience.' In 3-4 sentences, evaluate this proposal.
successCriteria: Identifies that this re-couples modules that were split apart for independence
Proposes at least one of the three alternatives (no sharing, minimal shared event bus, URL-based sharing)

rubric.excellent: Clearly explains that a shared global store undermines the independence micro-frontends were adopted for, and proposes a specific, appropriately scoped alternative with justification.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the coupling risk but the proposed alternative is vague or not clearly justified.
rubric.weak: Accepts the shared global store proposal as reasonable 'for convenience' without identifying the coupling risk.

errorPatterns.description: Learner accepts cross-module shared global state without recognizing the re-coupling risk to a micro-frontend architecture.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s4-state-management-architecture cross-app state-sharing options section.

questions.prompt: A team has published a well-built shared component library but has no design tokens, no governance process, and no versioning strategy. What is the most likely outcome over time?
options: Nothing - a component library alone is sufficient to keep teams visually and functionally consistent
Consuming teams will gradually drift out of sync and may fork components locally as review/update processes prove inadequate
The library will automatically stay synchronized because all teams use the same package
The lack of governance will have no effect on release velocity for any team

optionFeedback.feedback: This ignores that missing tokens/governance/distribution causes drift over time regardless of initial build quality.
Correct. Without governance and distribution, consuming teams drift and may fork locally.
Using the same package doesn't guarantee anyone actually upgrades to new versions.
A central team without a governance model typically becomes a bottleneck, directly affecting velocity for consuming teams.

successCriteria: Identifies drift/forking as the likely outcome

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats a component library alone as a complete design system.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s5-design-systems-shared-platform governance worked example.

errorPatterns.description: Learner assumes shared packages stay synchronized without an active distribution/versioning strategy.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s5-design-systems-shared-platform distribution-and-versioning section.

questions.prompt: A company uses independently deployed micro-frontends. In 2-3 sentences, recommend a distribution strategy (versioned package vs. live/shared-runtime) for their design system and justify it against their architecture.
successCriteria: Recommends versioned-package distribution
Justifies by matching the existing independence model of the micro-frontend architecture

rubric.excellent: Recommends versioned-package distribution and explicitly ties the justification to matching the teams' existing independent deploy cadence.
rubric.adequate: Recommends versioned-package distribution but the justification doesn't clearly connect to the existing architecture's independence model.
rubric.weak: Recommends live/shared-runtime distribution without acknowledging the loss of release-timing control this creates for independently deployed teams.

errorPatterns.description: Learner recommends a distribution strategy without considering the fit against the existing architecture style.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s5-design-systems-shared-platform distribution-strategy-to-architecture-style mapping.

questions.prompt: A team discovers post-launch that their micro-frontend-composed page ships five separate copies of the same UI framework, blowing their JS budget. What does this reveal?
options: The team simply needs a more aggressive minification/optimization pass
The composition strategy didn't account for shared/singleton dependency configuration, and needs to be revisited
This is expected and unavoidable in any micro-frontend architecture
The problem is unrelated to architecture and should be handled purely by the performance team after launch

optionFeedback.feedback: Minification cannot deduplicate five independently bundled copies of the same framework; this is a structural composition problem.
Correct. Performance budgets trace back to structural composition decisions, not late-stage optimization.
It's avoidable with proper shared-dependency configuration in the composition layer.
This directly restates the misconception that performance is purely a post-launch, non-architectural concern.

successCriteria: Identifies the composition strategy as the root cause

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats a structural performance problem as solvable through late-stage optimization alone.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s6-performance-scalability-resilience retail-app budget-traceback worked example.

questions.prompt: A page scores 98/100 on Lighthouse in CI, but real users on low-end devices report it feels slow. In 3-4 sentences, explain this gap and what data source should supplement the lab score.
successCriteria: Explains that lab scores don't reflect real device/network diversity
Names RUM (Real User Monitoring) as the supplementary data source

rubric.excellent: Clearly explains the lab-vs-field data gap and specifically names RUM as the ongoing source of truth, correctly framing lab scores as a useful but insufficient CI gate.
rubric.adequate: Identifies that lab scores are incomplete but doesn't name RUM or a comparable field-data solution specifically.
rubric.weak: Dismisses the lab score as meaningless, or attributes the gap solely to user devices with no proposed monitoring solution.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats a single lab performance score as a sufficient, complete performance signal.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s6-performance-scalability-resilience lab-vs-RUM section.

questions.prompt: Which cross-cutting concern is the strongest candidate for centralization in a shared platform layer?
options: A promotional banner's exact copy and imagery for one specific product line
Authentication session handling across all consuming applications
The internal file-naming convention used inside one team's codebase
A single team's internal sprint planning process

optionFeedback.feedback: This is product-specific and fails the centralization test; it should stay local to the owning team.
Correct. Inconsistent auth handling is a genuine security risk, passing the centralization test directly.
This has no cross-team user-visible or security impact and doesn't need central platform ownership.
Entirely unrelated to user-visible consistency or security/compliance risk.

successCriteria: Selects auth session handling as the centralization candidate

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats a cross-cutting concern as deferrable 'plumbing' rather than requiring upfront planning.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s6-performance-scalability-resilience cross-cutting-concerns-at-scale section.

questions.prompt: Four independently deployed micro-frontends each implement their own error logging in a different format. During an incident, engineers cannot determine whether the whole user journey is affected. In 3-4 sentences, explain the root cause and propose a fix.
successCriteria: Identifies the lack of a shared observability convention as the root cause
Proposes a shared logging/tracing convention while still allowing per-team dashboards

rubric.excellent: Clearly identifies the lack of a shared logging/tracing convention as the root cause and proposes a specific platform-level convention that preserves team-level dashboard ownership.
rubric.adequate: Identifies that inconsistency is the problem but the proposed fix is vague about what should be standardized.
rubric.weak: Attributes the incident-diagnosis failure to something other than the lack of a shared observability convention (e.g., 'the modules just need better individual logging').

errorPatterns.description: Learner assumes independent per-module implementation of a cross-cutting concern is sufficient without coordination.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s6-performance-scalability-resilience observability-coordination discussion.

questions.prompt: An architect proposes a technically 'ideal' micro-frontend split for a single team of 8 engineers with no plans to divide into separate teams. What's the flaw in this proposal?
options: There is no flaw; the most technically ideal architecture should always be adopted regardless of team structure
The org structure doesn't yet justify the coordination overhead the split would introduce, since there's no autonomy to be gained without genuinely separate teams
Micro-frontends automatically create team autonomy, so this proposal is beneficial regardless of current team structure
The flaw is unrelated to team structure and is purely a technical implementation risk

optionFeedback.feedback: This ignores that Conway's Law runs in both directions - existing org structure constrains what architectures are realistically adoptable.
Correct. Without genuinely separate teams, the coordination overhead of a split has no offsetting autonomy benefit.
This is the misconception that micro-frontends automatically enable autonomy, regardless of contracts and team structure.
The flaw is specifically about team structure mismatch, not a purely technical implementation concern.

successCriteria: Identifies the mismatch between org structure and proposed architecture

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner evaluates architecture purely on technical merit without considering whether current team structure supports it.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs Conway's Law both-directions discussion.

errorPatterns.description: Learner assumes decomposition automatically grants autonomy without contracts or platform investment.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface team-autonomy-without-contracts case from s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs.

questions.prompt: A company splits into four micro-frontend teams but reports increased cross-team dependency and slower releases, not more autonomy. In 3-4 sentences, diagnose the likely cause and propose a fix.
successCriteria: Identifies missing contracts and/or shared platform investment as the likely cause
Proposes a specific fix (e.g., explicit contracts, shared conventions, platform team investment)

rubric.excellent: Correctly diagnoses missing contracts or shared platform investment as the cause of increased coupling, and proposes a specific, actionable fix.
rubric.adequate: Identifies that something is missing but the diagnosis or fix is vague.
rubric.weak: Concludes the split itself was the wrong decision without diagnosing the actual missing ingredient (contracts/platform investment).

errorPatterns.description: Learner assumes decomposition alone should have produced autonomy and fails to diagnose the missing contract/platform layer.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s2-architecture-styles-tradeoffs and s5-design-systems-shared-platform governance material together.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best distinguishes an effective ADR from a simple summary of a decision?
options: An effective ADR uses more diagrams than a summary
An effective ADR documents the alternatives considered and the specific, context-based reasons each was rejected
An effective ADR is always longer than a summary
An effective ADR avoids discussing anything that was not ultimately chosen

optionFeedback.feedback: Diagram count isn't the distinguishing factor; the content of the reasoning is.
Correct. Most of an ADR's future value lives in the rejected-alternatives section.
Length isn't the criterion; a long document could still lack rejection reasoning.
This directly contradicts the core point; omitting rejected alternatives is exactly the failure mode being warned against.

successCriteria: Identifies rejected-alternatives reasoning as the distinguishing factor

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats a decision summary (chosen option only) as equivalent to a complete ADR.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s7-documenting-deciding-capstone ADR anatomy section and require rewriting a summary-only example.

questions.prompt: Write a short ADR (context, decision, at least two rejected alternatives with context-specific reasoning, and consequences) for a rendering-strategy decision on a content-heavy site of your choosing that is NOT the marketing-site example used in the course materials.
successCriteria: Includes all five ADR sections (title/status, context, decision, alternatives considered and rejected, consequences)
Rejection reasoning is specific to the chosen scenario, not generic claims about the alternatives
Diagram/boundary description (if included) specifies ownership and data-flow direction

rubric.excellent: All five ADR sections present; each rejected alternative has reasoning tied to specific facts of the chosen scenario (not generic statements like 'X is slower'); consequences section identifies at least one real future constraint created by the decision.
rubric.adequate: ADR structure is mostly complete but at least one rejected alternative uses generic rather than context-specific reasoning.
rubric.weak: Missing the rejected-alternatives section entirely, or all reasoning is generic and not tied to the specific scenario chosen.

errorPatterns.description: Learner writes generic rejection reasoning (e.g., 'SSR is slower') rather than reasoning tied to the specific scenario's facts.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s7-documenting-deciding-capstone marketing-site ADR worked example and highlight its context-specific rejection language.

questions.prompt: A senior engineer proposes the 'most flexible, most technology-rich' architecture for a small, well-understood project, reasoning that it will be 'safer' for the unknown future. What does the trade-off synthesis principle say about this reasoning?
options: It's correct - more flexibility is always the safer choice regardless of context
It's flawed - optionality has a real, ongoing complexity and cognitive-load cost that must be weighed against the actual, evidenced needs of the project
It's correct only if the team has fewer than ten engineers
It's irrelevant, since architecture decisions should never consider the future at all

optionFeedback.feedback: This is the misconception directly - more flexibility is not automatically safer regardless of context.
Correct. Optionality has a real complexity cost that must be weighed against actual, evidenced need, not assumed as free insurance.
Team size isn't the deciding factor here; the mismatch between complexity cost and actual current constraints is.
The principle doesn't argue against considering the future - it argues against defaulting to maximum flexibility as a substitute for evidence-based reasoning.

successCriteria: Rejects 'more flexibility is always safer' as a universal rule

rubric.excellent: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.adequate: N/A - auto-scored
rubric.weak: N/A - auto-scored

errorPatterns.description: Learner defaults to a maximally flexible architecture as inherently safer without weighing its complexity cost against actual need.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s7-documenting-deciding-capstone trade-off-synthesis-is-the-actual-skill section.

questions.prompt: Given a novel scenario (a regional grocery delivery startup: 20 engineers on one team today, planning to split into 3 teams within a year, moderate SEO dependency for store-locator pages, personalized order-tracking for logged-in users, strict uptime needs during peak delivery windows), produce a brief end-to-end architecture recommendation covering structure, rendering, data/state, and one explicitly rejected alternative for your structural choice.
successCriteria: Recommends a structure appropriate to a team that is not yet split (e.g., modular monolith, planning module boundaries ahead of the future split)
Assigns rendering strategy per route type (store-locator vs. order-tracking) with justification
Explicitly rejects at least one plausible alternative with scenario-specific (not generic) reasoning
Synthesizes at least three of the course's competency areas into one coherent proposal

rubric.excellent: Produces a coherent, internally consistent proposal spanning structure, rendering, and data/state, explicitly ties each choice to this scenario's specific facts (team timeline, SEO need, personalization, uptime), and rejects at least one alternative with scenario-specific reasoning rather than a generic claim.
rubric.adequate: Covers most of the required areas with reasonable choices, but at least one decision is asserted without clear scenario-specific justification, or the rejected-alternative reasoning is generic.
rubric.weak: Proposes a single architecture style/pattern applied uniformly without adapting to the scenario's specific facts, or omits the required rejected-alternative reasoning entirely.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes a single 'best practice' architecture without adapting reasoning to this scenario's specific team-timeline, SEO, and personalization facts.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface s7-documenting-deciding-capstone seven-step synthesis sequence and require the learner to explicitly cite which scenario fact drove each step.

glossary.definition: The practice of deciding how a front-end codebase, its runtime behavior, and its data flows are structured so that multiple teams, over multiple years, can ship reliably.
A statement of what a system must do (e.g., 'users can check out as guests').
A constraint on how well a system must perform a function - performance, scale, SEO, compliance, accessibility, offline support, device targets.
A choice that is expensive to reverse and affects multiple teams or the whole codebase, as opposed to a local implementation choice.
The full set of non-functional requirements and business facts that legitimately narrow available architecture options.
A single deployable application, single codebase, single build pipeline.
A single deployable application whose internal code is organized into strongly bounded, team-owned modules with enforced boundaries, without separate deployment.
An architecture where independently deployable front-end units are composed (at build time, runtime, or edge) into a single user-facing experience.
A Webpack/Rspack capability allowing separately built JavaScript bundles to share code and load each other's modules at runtime.
The observation that systems tend to mirror the communication structure of the organizations that build them, in both directions.
The mechanism by which independently built front-end pieces are assembled into one experience - at build time, client runtime, or server/edge.
A rendering approach where the browser downloads a mostly empty HTML shell and JavaScript, then renders content client-side.
A rendering approach where the server renders full HTML per request, sent ready-to-paint to the browser, then hydrated.
A rendering approach where HTML is rendered ahead of time at build time and served as static files.
A rendering approach where statically generated pages are regenerated in the background after deployment, on a schedule or on-demand, without a full rebuild.
A rendering refinement where the server sends HTML progressively, and/or ships JavaScript only for interactive 'islands' within an otherwise static page.
The process of attaching interactive JavaScript behavior to server-rendered HTML already present in the DOM.
A backend layer built and owned specifically to shape data for one front-end client's needs, sitting between that client and one or more underlying services.
The question of which team is responsible for defining and maintaining the shape of a data contract (API schema) between front end and back end.
State that exists only to drive the interface and has no independent source of truth elsewhere - e.g., whether a dropdown is open.
A local copy of data whose true source of truth lives on the server, which can go stale and must be invalidated/refetched rather than simply 'set.'
The condition where cached data no longer matches the server's current truth.
State accessible from anywhere in an application, as opposed to state scoped to one component or feature.
Passing or synchronizing state across independently deployed front-end modules, relevant specifically in micro-frontend architectures.
The principle that any given piece of data should have exactly one authoritative origin, with all other copies treated as derived or cached.
A named, platform-agnostic value (e.g., color.brand.primary, spacing.md) representing a design decision, decoupled from any specific component's implementation.
A low-level, highly reusable UI building block (button, input, text) with minimal business logic.
A higher-level component built from primitives, often encoding a shared product-specific pattern (e.g., a product card).
The process and decision rights that determine who can propose, approve, and merge changes to a shared system.
How a shared library's updates actually reach consuming applications - e.g., published versioned packages vs. a live shared runtime dependency.
A versioning convention (major.minor.patch) signaling the nature of a change to communicate breaking vs. non-breaking updates to consumers.
Google's standardized set of user-experience metrics - including loading, interactivity, and visual stability - used as real-world performance signals.
A pre-agreed limit (e.g., max JavaScript bundle size, max Time to Interactive) that a team commits to staying under, treated as a hard constraint during development.
Dividing a JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand, rather than shipping the entire application's code on first load.
Performance data collected from actual users' real sessions in production, as opposed to synthetic lab tests.
A runtime toggle allowing a feature to be enabled, disabled, or partially rolled out without a new deployment.
A mechanism that catches rendering errors in part of a UI tree and prevents them from crashing the entire application.
A short, structured document capturing one architectural decision, its context, the alternatives considered, and the reasoning for the final choice.
A proposal document circulated for team feedback before a decision is finalized, often used for larger or more contested architectural changes.
The explicit line marking what is owned or controlled by one team or module versus another.
The explicit indication, in a diagram, of which way information moves between components, including which system is the source of truth.
The act of weighing multiple competing architectural forces against a specific context to reach and justify one decision.

sections.title: Foundations & Requirements Elicitation for Front-End Architecture
sections.description: Establishes what 'front-end system design' means at staff/architect level and how to translate business context into architecture-relevant requirements before any structural decision is made.

learningObjectives.description: Distinguish front-end system design from feature-level engineering by identifying at least three architecture-level decisions a senior IC would not normally own.
Elicit and categorize functional vs. non-functional requirements from a stated business scenario.
Map elicited requirements to the categories of architectural decisions they will later constrain (structure, rendering, data, state).

motivationHooks.prompt: A feature engineer optimizes a component. An architect optimizes a system — and systems fail for reasons no single component can fix: a rendering choice that can't scale past three teams, a state pattern that silently duplicates data, a 'quick' micro-frontend split that triples deploy time. Architecture mistakes are expensive to reverse, which is exactly why this course starts before any code gets written.

Before you read further: two companies both describe themselves as building a 'content-heavy web application.' Would you expect their correct front-end architectures to look similar? By the end of this section you'll be able to explain, precisely, why the honest answer is usually no.

Every later section in this course — structure, rendering, state, shared platform, performance, documentation — takes its inputs from the requirements work you do here. Get this step wrong, and every downstream decision inherits the error.

furtherReading.rationaleNote: Foundational framing of architectural vs. non-architectural decisions, applicable well beyond the back end.

furtherReading.title: Yagni
furtherReading.author: Martin Fowler
furtherReading.rationaleNote: On the discipline of not over-engineering ahead of real, evidenced constraints — the flip side of the 'when to stop eliciting' discussion in this section.

furtherReading.title: Rendering on the Web
furtherReading.author: web.dev (Google)
furtherReading.rationaleNote: Useful preview of how the NFRs you elicit here (SEO, freshness) will concretely constrain rendering strategy in Section 3.

furtherReading.title: The cost of architectural reversal: replatforming case studies
