learningExperiences.title: Performance Budget Trace-Back: Regional Airline Booking Search Results

contentBlocks.content: WHY THIS MATTERS: This exercise re-examines the airline booking flow's rendering and composition decisions from Sections 2 and 3, but this time under a specific performance budget. If your earlier decisions don't hold up under this lens, that's the point of the exercise — it's exactly the re-evaluation this section is designed to force.

selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: The regional airline booking flow's search-results page, drawing on the composition strategy from Section 2 and the rendering strategy from Section 3's per-route mapping exercise, with the platform's known heavy mobile traffic from low-bandwidth regions.
selfGuidedExercise.objective: Given a performance budget for the booking search-results page, trace it back to at least two specific decisions from earlier sections that would need to change or be confirmed to hit it, and propose one caching or code-splitting technique specific to your chosen composition strategy.
instructions: You're given a target: initial JS payload under 200KB and LCP under 2.2s on the booking search-results page.

Revisit your Section 2 composition-strategy decision for the booking module: does it account for shared/singleton dependency configuration, or would it risk shipping duplicate framework copies as in the retail worked example?

Revisit your Section 3 rendering-strategy decision for the booking search-results route: does it deliver listing content in the initial HTML, or does it depend on a client-side round-trip that would jeopardize the 2.2s LCP target, especially given low-bandwidth mobile traffic?

Propose one specific caching or code-splitting technique matched to your composition strategy (e.g., shared-singleton configuration for client-side runtime composition, or edge fragment caching for edge/server-side composition).
State explicitly: does hitting this budget require confirming your earlier decisions, or changing them? Justify your answer.
materialsOrPrerequisites: Completion of the core lecture in this section
Your Section 2 composition-strategy decision and Section 3 rendering-strategy table for the airline scenario, if available
reflectionPrompts: Did tracing this budget back reveal a gap in your earlier composition or rendering decisions, or did they hold up as-is?
Given the low-bandwidth mobile traffic constraint from Section 1, was the 200KB/2.2s budget more or less achievable than you initially expected?

selfAssessmentCriteria: I traced the budget back to at least two specific earlier decisions (composition and rendering), not just general performance advice
My proposed caching/code-splitting technique is specific to my chosen composition strategy, not a generic 'use code-splitting' statement
I explicitly stated whether my earlier decisions needed to change and why

learningExperiences.title: Cross-Cutting Coordination Plan: Regional Airline Booking Platform

contentBlocks.content: WHY THIS MATTERS: In Section 5 you applied the centralization test to design-system-adjacent concerns for this airline scenario. Here you apply the same test to a different, operational set of concerns — observability, error boundaries, and feature flags — as a direct check on whether the underlying heuristic transferred, rather than being memorized as a one-time answer.

selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: The regional airline booking platform's four teams (booking, check-in, loyalty, content/marketing), with strict uptime requirements during weather disruptions established in Section 1.
selfGuidedExercise.objective: Plan a coordinated approach to observability, error boundaries, and feature flagging across the airline's independently owned modules.
instructions: Propose a shared observability convention (logging/tracing format) that would let engineers trace an incident across the booking and check-in modules during a weather-disruption traffic spike, while still letting each team own its own dashboards.
Propose an error-boundary convention for the composed booking search-results page, specifying what should happen if the recommendations or loyalty-promotion fragment fails to render.

Propose a feature-flag coordination approach for a scenario where a redesign touches both the booking header (owned by the booking team) and the loyalty badge in that header (owned by the loyalty team), so users don't see a half-rolled-out inconsistent state.
materialsOrPrerequisites: Completion of the core lecture in this section
Your Section 5 centralization decisions for this scenario, if available
reflectionPrompts: Did applying the centralization test to observability, error boundaries, and feature flags feel more or less clear-cut than applying it to design-system concerns in Section 5?
Given the strict uptime requirement during weather disruptions from Section 1, which of your three proposals here feels most safety-critical, and why?

selfAssessmentCriteria: My observability proposal explicitly balances shared convention with per-team dashboard ownership
My error-boundary proposal specifies a defined fallback, not just 'catch the error'
My feature-flag proposal explicitly addresses cross-team consistency for the header/badge scenario

sections.title: Documenting, Communicating & Deciding: The Architecture Capstone
sections.description: Synthesizes all prior competencies into the deliverable a staff engineer actually produces: a defensible, documented architecture decision, communicated through an ADR and a labeled diagram.

learningObjectives.description: Author an ADR that captures a specific architectural decision, the alternatives considered, and the rejection rationale for each.
Produce an architecture diagram and accompanying narrative that communicates system boundaries, ownership, and data flow to a mixed technical/non-technical audience.
Given a full scenario, synthesize decisions across structure, rendering, data, state, and shared platform into one coherent, trade-off-justified proposal.

motivationHooks.prompt: An architecture that lives only in your head is not an architecture — it's a hunch that happens to compile. The deliverable that actually makes you a staff-level architect isn't the diagram in a slide deck; it's a documented decision that a new hire can read in twenty minutes and understand not just what was chosen, but why, and what was deliberately rejected.

If a summary of the chosen option were as valuable as a full ADR, nobody would bother writing the 'alternatives considered and rejected' section. Experienced teams write it anyway, every time. By the end of this section you'll be able to say exactly why that section carries most of the document's future value.

Every scenario you've worked through in this course — the furniture retailer, the airline, the ride-sharing app, the design-system governance model — was building toward this moment. This section asks you to do what a staff architect actually does: take everything you've decided, and everything you rejected, and put it into a form someone else can act on.

learningExperiences.title: Documenting, Communicating & Deciding: The Architecture Capstone

contentBlocks.content:
Learning objectives for this lecture:
1. Author an ADR that captures a specific architectural decision, the alternatives considered, and the rejection rationale for each.
2. Produce an architecture diagram and accompanying narrative that communicates system boundaries, ownership, and data flow to a mixed technical/non-technical audience.
3. Given a full scenario, synthesize decisions across structure, rendering, data, state, and shared platform into one coherent, trade-off-justified proposal.

An architecture that lives only in your head is not an architecture — it's a hunch that happens to compile. The deliverable that actually makes you a staff-level architect isn't the diagram in a slide deck; it's a documented decision that a new hire can read in twenty minutes and understand not just what was chosen, but why, and what was deliberately rejected. This section is where every competency from Sections 1–6 converges into that single artifact.

KEY TERMS & DEFINITIONS
- ADR (Architecture Decision Record): a short, structured document capturing one architectural decision, its context, the alternatives considered, and the reasoning for the final choice.
- RFC (Request for Comments): a proposal document circulated for team feedback before a decision is finalized, often used for larger or more contested architectural changes.
- System boundary: the explicit line marking what is owned/controlled by one team or module versus another.
- Data-flow direction: the explicit indication, in a diagram, of which way information moves between components (e.g., which system is the source of truth, which is a consumer).
- Trade-off synthesis: the act of weighing multiple competing architectural forces against a specific context to reach and justify one decision.

1. DOCUMENTATION IS NOT A SUMMARY WRITTEN AFTER THE FACT
The misconception to retire first: documentation is a summary written after the decision is made. A summary tells a reader what you chose. An effective ADR tells them what you didn't choose, and why — and this is where most of an ADR's future value lives. Six months from now, when someone proposes revisiting a decision, the value isn't re-explaining the chosen option; it's being able to say 'we considered X, and rejected it because of Y, which is still true' — or realizing Y is no longer true, and the decision should be revisited. Without the rejected-alternatives section, every ADR degrades into an unfalsifiable 'trust us' document.

2. ANATOMY OF AN EFFECTIVE ADR
A working structure, deliberately short enough that people actually write and read them:
- Title & status (proposed / accepted / superseded).
- Context: the requirements and constraints (this is where Section 1's elicitation work becomes visible in the artifact) that make this decision necessary now.
- Decision: the specific choice, stated plainly.
- Alternatives considered and rejected: each alternative, with one to two sentences on why it was rejected — specific to this context, not a generic statement of the alternative's general weaknesses.
- Consequences: what this decision makes easier, what it makes harder, and what future decisions it constrains.

4. DIAGRAMS THAT ACTUALLY COMMUNICATE, NOT JUST ILLUSTRATE
The second misconception: a diagram is self-explanatory. A box-and-arrow diagram without explicit system boundaries (who owns what), explicit data-flow direction (which way information moves, and which system is the source of truth), and a short accompanying narrative creates the illusion of alignment while different readers silently interpret it differently. A useful discipline: every diagram should be able to answer, at a glance, 'if this breaks, whose pager goes off' and 'if I change this box, whose review do I need' — if it can't, the boundaries aren't explicit enough yet.

5. THE CAPSTONE SYNTHESIS: BRINGING EVERY COMPETENCY TOGETHER
A full architecture proposal for a real scenario needs to visibly draw on every prior section, in the following practical sequence:
1. Elicit and categorize requirements (Section 1) — functional and non-functional, identifying which are decision-critical.
2. Choose a structural style and, if applicable, composition strategy (Section 2), justified against team topology now and 12–18 months out.
3. Assign rendering strategy per route and design the data-access layer (Section 3), justified against the SEO/freshness/personalization needs surfaced in step 1.
4. Design the state architecture (Section 4), distinguishing client/UI from server-cache state and resolving cross-module sharing if step 2 chose micro-frontends.
5. Define the shared platform layer (Section 5) — design system structure, governance, and which cross-cutting concerns are centralized.
6. Set and trace performance budgets and cross-cutting operational concerns (Section 6) back to the structural and rendering choices made in steps 2–3.
7. Write the ADR and diagram (this section) — documenting the decision, alternatives rejected at each step, and the consequences.

6. TRADE-OFF SYNTHESIS IS THE ACTUAL SKILL BEING TESTED
The final misconception to name directly, because it undercuts everything else if unaddressed: there is no single 'best practice' architecture that applies regardless of context, and more technology/flexibility is not always safer — optionality (extra composition mechanisms, extra state-sharing layers, extra abstraction) has a real ongoing cost in complexity and cognitive load that must be weighed against the flexibility it buys. The mark of a senior architect is not knowing the most patterns — it's correctly matching a small number of well-understood patterns to a specific context's actual constraints, and being able to say precisely why a fashionable alternative was rejected for this case.

COMMON ERRORS CALL-OUT BOX
⚠️ Common Error 1: Writing an ADR that only documents the chosen option. Without documented, context-specific rejection reasoning for the alternatives, the ADR can't be evaluated or revisited later — it becomes an assertion, not a record of reasoning.
⚠️ Common Error 2: Producing a diagram with boxes and arrows but no ownership or data-flow-direction labels. This creates false alignment: two readers can walk away with different mental models of who owns what.
⚠️ Common Error 3: Defaulting to more flexible/general architecture 'to be safe.' Extra optionality has a real, ongoing complexity cost; the safer choice is usually the one that most precisely matches the actual, current, evidenced constraints — not the one with the most future options built in.

interactionSequence.prompt: Which of the following best distinguishes an effective ADR from a simple summary of a decision?
options: An effective ADR uses more diagrams than a summary
An effective ADR documents the alternatives considered and the specific, context-based reasons each was rejected
An effective ADR is always longer than a summary
An effective ADR avoids discussing anything that was not ultimately chosen

optionFeedback.feedback: Diagram count isn't the distinguishing factor identified in the lecture; the content of the reasoning is.
Correct - matches the lecture's core point that most of an ADR's future value lives in the rejected-alternatives section.
Length isn't the criterion; a summary could be long and still lack rejection reasoning, or an ADR could be short and complete.
This directly contradicts the lecture's central point; omitting rejected alternatives is exactly the failure mode being warned against.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: Six months from now, the value of an ADR isn't re-explaining what was chosen — it's being able to say 'we considered X and rejected it because of Y, which is still true' (or isn't). That's only possible if the alternatives were documented in the first place.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Check whether an ADR explains what wasn't chosen and why, not just what was chosen.
hints: What would a reader need six months from now to re-evaluate this decision?
What happens to an ADR that only documents the winning option?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in this answer?

interactionSequence.prompt: A senior engineer proposes the 'most flexible, most technology-rich' architecture for a small, well-understood project, reasoning that it will be 'safer' for the unknown future. What does the trade-off synthesis principle say about this reasoning?
options: It's correct - more flexibility is always the safer choice regardless of context
It's flawed - optionality has a real, ongoing complexity and cognitive-load cost that must be weighed against the actual, evidenced needs of the project
It's correct only if the team has fewer than ten engineers
It's irrelevant, since architecture decisions should never consider the future at all

optionFeedback.feedback: This is the exact misconception the lecture names and rejects.
Correct - matches the lecture's explicit rejection of 'more flexibility is always safer' as a universal rule.
Team size isn't the deciding factor in the lecture's framing; the mismatch between complexity cost and actual current constraints is.
The lecture doesn't argue against considering the future; it argues against defaulting to maximum flexibility as a substitute for evidence-based reasoning.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: Optionality isn't free — extra composition mechanisms, extra state-sharing layers, and extra abstraction all carry ongoing complexity cost. The mark of a senior architect is matching a small number of well-understood patterns to actual, evidenced constraints, not maximizing future options.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Weigh flexibility's complexity cost against the project's actual, current, evidenced needs — don't treat flexibility as free insurance.
hints: Does more flexibility ever cost anything, or is it purely upside?
What does a small, well-understood project actually need right now?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in this answer?

workedExamples.problem: A B2B SaaS company's marketing site needs to move off pure CSR because organic search traffic (a stated NFR from their Section-1-style elicitation) is underperforming competitors. Write an ADR for the rendering-strategy decision.
steps: Decision: Adopt SSG for the marketing site, with ISR for the blog/case-studies section (content updates a few times a week), keeping the authenticated app dashboard as CSR.

Alternative 1 - Full SSR for everything: rejected because the marketing site's content changes rarely enough that paying a per-request server cost for pages that could be pre-rendered once is unjustified; would have also added infrastructure complexity disproportionate to the actual freshness need.

Alternative 2 - Leave everything as CSR and rely on client-side-rendering-aware crawling: rejected because too much risk given the explicit, measured underperformance in organic search already observed; the SEO NFR is a hard constraint, not a nice-to-have.

Alternative 3 - Full site migration to SSG, including the dashboard: rejected because the dashboard is authenticated, personalized, and not indexable by search engines in the first place, so pre-rendering it offers no SEO benefit and would force unnecessary rebuild-on-every-change complexity for user-specific data.

Consequences: content authors get a documented publish-to-live latency (ISR regeneration window) they need to be told about; the marketing team gains fast, cheap, reliably indexable pages; a future decision to add personalization to marketing pages would need to revisit this ADR, since SSG doesn't support per-user personalization without additional architecture.
workedExamples.conclusion: Notice that every rejected alternative is justified by this company's specific context (their SEO underperformance, their content update cadence, their dashboard's auth requirement) — not by a generic claim like 'SSR is slower.' This is what separates a defensible ADR from a preference statement.

retrievalPractice.prompt: What section of an ADR carries most of its long-term value, and why?
Name the seven-step capstone synthesis sequence and which section each step draws from.
Why is 'more flexibility is always safer' false as an architectural principle?

transferExercises.scenario: A city planning department is deciding between building a new dedicated bus lane versus widening a general-purpose road.
transferExercises.task: Write the 'alternatives considered and rejected' section of an ADR-style document for this decision, using context-specific reasoning (not generic claims) the way the marketing-site example did.
successCriteria: Rejects at least one alternative with reasoning tied to this city's specific facts (e.g., measured congestion data, ridership projections), not a generic claim like 'bus lanes are always better'
Includes at least two distinct alternatives with separate, specific rejection reasoning
Avoids reproducing the marketing-site example's reasoning verbatim in a different domain

transferExercises.scenario: A manufacturer is choosing between a single centralized inventory-management system and separate per-factory systems that sync periodically.
transferExercises.task: Sketch (in words) what a diagram for this decision would need to make explicit (boundaries, ownership, data-flow direction) to avoid the 'diagram is self-explanatory' trap.
successCriteria: Specifies what system boundary would need to be drawn (e.g., where centralized inventory data ends and per-factory data begins)
Specifies ownership for at least two boxes in the described diagram
Specifies data-flow direction, including which system is the source of truth for inventory counts

learningExperiences.title: ADR Writing Workshop: Regional Airline Booking Platform

contentBlocks.content: WHY THIS MATTERS: This workshop asks you to write a real ADR for the single highest-stakes decision you've made across the airline scenario in Sections 1–6. This is deliberately narrower than the full capstone proposal that follows — the goal here is depth on one decision, written the way it would actually need to be defended to a skeptical colleague six months from now.

selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: The regional airline booking platform scenario, spanning every decision made across Sections 1–6: requirements, structure/composition, rendering/data, state, shared platform, and performance/cross-cutting concerns.
selfGuidedExercise.objective: Write one complete ADR, using the five-part structure from this section, for the single decision from the airline scenario you consider highest-stakes.
instructions: Select the single decision from your work across Sections 2, 3, 4, or 5 that you consider highest-stakes for this airline platform, and briefly justify why you selected it over the others.
Write the Context section: the specific requirements and constraints from Section 1's elicitation that make this decision necessary.
Write the Decision section: state your choice plainly.

Write the Alternatives Considered and Rejected section: name at least two alternatives you didn't choose, and for each, write one to two sentences of rejection reasoning specific to this airline's facts — not a generic claim about the alternative.
Write the Consequences section: what this decision makes easier, what it makes harder, and what future decision it constrains.
Self-check: reread your rejection reasoning and confirm it couldn't be copy-pasted unchanged into an ADR for a completely different company. If it could, it's too generic.
materialsOrPrerequisites: Completion of the core lecture in this section
Your prior decisions and reasoning from Sections 1 through 6 for the airline scenario
reflectionPrompts: Which decision did you select as highest-stakes, and what made it stand out from the others?
Did writing out the rejected alternatives change your confidence in the decision you originally made, or reveal a gap in your original reasoning?
Which part of the five-part structure was hardest to write, and why?

selfAssessmentCriteria: My ADR includes all five sections: title/status, context, decision, alternatives considered and rejected, consequences
My rejection reasoning for each alternative is specific to this airline's facts, not a generic statement about the alternative
My consequences section names at least one real future constraint this decision creates

learningExperiences.title: Capstone: Full Architecture Proposal — Regional Grocery Delivery Startup

contentBlocks.content: WHY THIS MATTERS: This is the final deliverable of the course, and deliberately uses a scenario you haven't seen before — a regional grocery delivery startup — rather than extending the airline scenario further. A staff architect's real value isn't reciting a memorized answer for one familiar company; it's applying the same seven-step synthesis sequence to a genuinely new context and reaching a defensible, context-specific conclusion.

selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: A regional grocery delivery startup: 20 engineers on one team today, planning to split into 3 teams within a year (likely along the lines of store-browsing/ordering, delivery logistics/tracking, and account/loyalty), moderate SEO dependency for store-locator pages, personalized order-tracking for logged-in users, and strict uptime needs during peak delivery windows.
selfGuidedExercise.objective: Produce a complete architecture proposal following the seven-step synthesis sequence from this section, culminating in one full ADR and a labeled diagram description.
instructions: Step 1 - Requirements: write a short requirements summary (functional and non-functional), flagging which non-functional requirements are decision-critical.
Step 2 - Structure: propose a structural/composition decision, justified against the team's timeline (1 team today, 3 teams within a year) rather than today's structure alone.

Step 3 - Rendering & data: produce a per-route rendering-strategy table (at minimum: store-locator pages, order-tracking dashboard) and a data-access layer design (BFF, GraphQL, or both), each justified against the requirements from Step 1.
Step 4 - State: propose a state-architecture plan, distinguishing client/UI from server-cache state, and specify a cross-module state-sharing approach if Step 2 chose a decomposed structure.
Step 5 - Shared platform: propose a design-system layering approach, a governance model sized to this team's timeline, and name which cross-cutting concerns you'd centralize.
Step 6 - Performance: propose two concrete performance budgets (e.g., a JS payload limit and an LCP target for a specific route), and trace each back to a specific decision made in Steps 2–3.

Step 7 - Document: write one full ADR (using the five-part structure) for the single decision you consider highest-stakes in this proposal, and write a short labeled-diagram description (boundaries, ownership, data-flow direction) for the overall system.

Final check: for at least one major decision, explicitly state which plausible alternative you rejected and why, using reasoning specific to this grocery-delivery scenario rather than a general architectural preference.
materialsOrPrerequisites: Completion of all prior sections in this course
A document or notes app to record your full proposal, since this exercise produces a multi-part deliverable
reflectionPrompts: Which step of the seven-step sequence took the most iteration before you were confident in your answer?
Did any decision from an earlier step force you to revisit a decision from a step before it? Which, and why?
If you had to defend this proposal to a skeptical VP in five minutes, which single piece of evidence from your requirements (Step 1) would you lead with?

selfAssessmentCriteria: My proposal visibly completes all seven steps, in order, each building on the ones before it
Every major decision is justified by a specific fact from this scenario, not a generic architectural preference
My ADR includes context-specific rejection reasoning for at least two alternatives
My diagram description explicitly specifies ownership and data-flow direction, not just boxes and arrows
I explicitly rejected at least one plausible alternative for my structural decision, with scenario-specific reasoning
